title: Contributing to savingBiocObjects
text: Thank interest improving documentation. Contributions kinds welcome: fixing typos, improving explanations, adding new sections, updating code examples Bioconductor ecosystem evolves.

title: Contributing to savingBiocObjects
text: Open issue first anything beyond minor fix — describe think missing wrong, can agree approach write. Fork repository create branch main. Edit vignette vignettes/savingBiocObjects.qmd. file standard Quarto markdown; code chunks reproducible. Preview locally pkgdown: Open pull request main. GitHub Actions workflow render site automatically; deployed preview published merge main.
code: pkgdown::build_site() # or, for just the vignette: pkgdown::build_article("savingBiocObjects")

title: Contributing to savingBiocObjects
text: concrete. Prefer runnable code examples prose-descriptions. State trade-offs. approach include honest disadvantages, just advantages. Keep recommendations date. new Bioconductor package supersedes older approach, note update summary table. Cite packages properly. Use citation("pkgname") link Bioconductor landing page.

title: Contributing to savingBiocObjects
text: project follows Bioconductor Code Conduct. Please respectful constructive interactions.

title: Contributing to savingBiocObjects
text: Open GitHub issue reach via Bioconductor support site.

title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: common question Bioinformatics workflows : save Bioconductor object collaborator can load use , persists reliably across time? answer depends several factors: Longevity: file need readable 5 10 years? Language interoperability: recipient use R, Python, ? Object size: object small enough serialize entirely, contain large -disk arrays? Reproducibility: saved form capture provenance metadata alongside data? vignette walks main options, trade-offs, recommendations common scenarios.

title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: simplest approach R’s built-binary serialization. saveRDS() saves single object .rds file; save() bundles one named objects .RData (.rda) file. Prefer saveRDS() use cases. save() / load() silently overwrites object calling environment shares name, common source confusion. main remaining use case save() .rda data files shipped inside R packages (data/). Advantages: Zero setup — works R object. Compression applied automatically (gzip default). Round-trips perfectly: loaded object identical saved one. Disadvantages: R-: Python languages read files without bridge library. Large objects loaded entirely RAM; lazy / -disk access. use : quick sharing R users project, saving intermediate objects pipeline, anything ~1 GB. workflow downloads .rds files remote URL, consider using BiocFileCache cache locally fetched . guarantee S4 object serialized today work older versions Bioconductor. common reasons S4 class defined earlier version, exists definition changed — new slots added, slots renamed removed, infrastructure moved packages. concrete example: recent Bioconductor release, Seqinfo moved GenomicRanges package. GRanges serialized machine newer setup loaded machine older GenomicRanges class definition embedded Seqinfo slot found. reverse also true: older GRanges objects loaded newer session sometimes required updateObject() migrate internal representation: general lesson : can save data format tied particular version Bioconductor — better, tied R — . GRanges, instance, simple TSV often enough: round-trip survives Bioconductor version readable Python command line. alabaster ecosystem (described ) applies principle systematically better support complex objects, using HDF5 JSON underlying storage formats.
code: library(SummarizedExperiment)  se <- SummarizedExperiment(   assays = list(counts = matrix(1:12, nrow = 3)),   colData = DataFrame(     condition = c("A", "A", "B", "B"),      row.names=1:4     ),   rowData = DataFrame(     gene = c("gene1","gene2","gene3"),      row.names=1:3     ) )  # Single object tmp_rds <- tempfile(fileext = ".rds") saveRDS(se, file = tmp_rds) se_from_rds <- readRDS(tmp_rds) se_from_rds class: SummarizedExperiment dim: 3 4 metadata(0): assays(1): counts rownames(3): 1 2 3 rowData names(1): gene colnames(4): 1 2 3 4 colData names(1): condition # Multiple objects in one file tmp_rda <- tempfile(fileext = ".RData") save(se, file = tmp_rda) load(tmp_rda)  # restores 'se' by name into the current environment # not evaluated gr <- readRDS("old_granges.rds") gr <- updateObject(gr, verbose = TRUE) library(GenomicRanges)  gr <- GRanges(   seqnames = "chr1",   ranges = IRanges(start = c(100, 200, 300), width = 50) ) names(gr) <- c("peak1", "peak2", "peak3") gr$score  <- c(500, 800, 300)   # standard BED score column gr$log2fc <- c(1.2, -0.5, 2.1) # extra metadata column  tmp_tsv <- tempfile(fileext = ".tsv") write.table(as.data.frame(gr), tmp_tsv, sep = "\t", quote = FALSE)  gr_from_tsv <- makeGRangesFromDataFrame(   read.table(tmp_tsv, header = TRUE, sep = "\t"),   keep.extra.columns = TRUE ) gr_from_tsv GRanges object with 3 ranges and 2 metadata columns:         seqnames    ranges strand |     score    log2fc            <Rle> <IRanges>  <Rle> | <integer> <numeric>   peak1     chr1   100-149      * |       500       1.2   peak2     chr1   200-249      * |       800      -0.5   peak3     chr1   300-349      * |       300       2.1   -------   seqinfo: 1 sequence from an unspecified genome; no seqlengths

title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: guarantee S4 object serialized today work older versions Bioconductor. common reasons S4 class defined earlier version, exists definition changed — new slots added, slots renamed removed, infrastructure moved packages. concrete example: recent Bioconductor release, Seqinfo moved GenomicRanges package. GRanges serialized machine newer setup loaded machine older GenomicRanges class definition embedded Seqinfo slot found. reverse also true: older GRanges objects loaded newer session sometimes required updateObject() migrate internal representation: general lesson : can save data format tied particular version Bioconductor — better, tied R — . GRanges, instance, simple TSV often enough: round-trip survives Bioconductor version readable Python command line. alabaster ecosystem (described ) applies principle systematically better support complex objects, using HDF5 JSON underlying storage formats.
code: # not evaluated gr <- readRDS("old_granges.rds") gr <- updateObject(gr, verbose = TRUE) library(GenomicRanges)  gr <- GRanges(   seqnames = "chr1",   ranges = IRanges(start = c(100, 200, 300), width = 50) ) names(gr) <- c("peak1", "peak2", "peak3") gr$score  <- c(500, 800, 300)   # standard BED score column gr$log2fc <- c(1.2, -0.5, 2.1) # extra metadata column  tmp_tsv <- tempfile(fileext = ".tsv") write.table(as.data.frame(gr), tmp_tsv, sep = "\t", quote = FALSE)  gr_from_tsv <- makeGRangesFromDataFrame(   read.table(tmp_tsv, header = TRUE, sep = "\t"),   keep.extra.columns = TRUE ) gr_from_tsv GRanges object with 3 ranges and 2 metadata columns:         seqnames    ranges strand |     score    log2fc            <Rle> <IRanges>  <Rle> | <integer> <numeric>   peak1     chr1   100-149      * |       500       1.2   peak2     chr1   200-249      * |       800      -0.5   peak3     chr1   300-349      * |       300       2.1   -------   seqinfo: 1 sequence from an unspecified genome; no seqlengths

title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: large assay matrices (e.g., single-cell count matrices millions cells), impractical hold entire object RAM. HDF5Array package provides array classes backed HDF5 files, enabling lazy loading --memory computation. saved directory contains HDF5 file assay data .rds file non-assay metadata. Advantages: Assay data stored disk; chunks access read RAM. HDF5 widely used binary format readers Python (h5py, anndata), Julia, C/C++, . Good objects tens gigabytes assay data. Disadvantages: output directory, single file, complicates transfer (use tar zip sharing). .rds envelope metadata still R-specific. Write performance can slower saveRDS() small objects. use : large single-cell spatial datasets want -disk access; workflows shared R users need memory efficiency. single-cell workflows move R Python, zellkonverter package converts directly SingleCellExperiment Python AnnData format (.h5ad files used scanpy related tools). Like HDF5Array, .h5ad HDF5-based format. often convenient path collaborators working scanpy, .h5ad native format side. trade-relative alabaster .h5ad AnnData-specific rather general Bioconductor serialization format.
code: library(HDF5Array)  tmp_hdf5 <- tempfile() saveHDF5SummarizedExperiment(se, dir = tmp_hdf5, replace = TRUE)  # Assay data remains on disk until accessed se_from_hdf5 <- loadHDF5SummarizedExperiment(tmp_hdf5) se_from_hdf5 class: SummarizedExperiment dim: 3 4 metadata(0): assays(1): counts rownames(3): 1 2 3 rowData names(1): gene colnames(4): 1 2 3 4 colData names(1): condition library(zellkonverter) library(SingleCellExperiment)  sce <- as(se, "SingleCellExperiment")  tmp_h5ad <- tempfile(fileext = ".h5ad") writeH5AD(sce, file = tmp_h5ad) Installing pyenv ... Done! pyenv has been installed to '/home/runner/.local/share/r-reticulate/pyenv/bin/pyenv'. Using Python: /home/runner/.pyenv/versions/3.14.0/bin/python3.14 Creating virtual environment '/home/runner/.cache/R/basilisk/1.24.0/zellkonverter/1.22.0/zellkonverterAnnDataEnv-0.12.3' ... Done! Installing packages: pip, wheel, setuptools Installing packages: 'anndata==0.12.3', 'h5py==3.15.1', 'natsort==8.4.0', 'numpy==2.3.4', 'pandas==2.3.3', 'scipy==1.16.2' Virtual environment '/home/runner/.cache/R/basilisk/1.24.0/zellkonverter/1.22.0/zellkonverterAnnDataEnv-0.12.3' successfully created. sce_from_h5ad <- readH5AD(tmp_h5ad) sce_from_h5ad class: SingleCellExperiment dim: 3 4 metadata(0): assays(1): counts rownames(3): 1 2 3 rowData names(1): gene colnames(4): 1 2 3 4 colData names(1): condition reducedDimNames(0): mainExpName: NULL altExpNames(0):

title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: single-cell workflows move R Python, zellkonverter package converts directly SingleCellExperiment Python AnnData format (.h5ad files used scanpy related tools). Like HDF5Array, .h5ad HDF5-based format. often convenient path collaborators working scanpy, .h5ad native format side. trade-relative alabaster .h5ad AnnData-specific rather general Bioconductor serialization format.

title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: alabaster family packages part broader ArtifactDB project, provides multi-language system storing retrieving analysis-ready Bioconductor objects. core idea save objects directories standard files (HDF5, JSON, CSV) whose format defined explicit, versioned specifications — meaning saved form readable without R, can evolve time without breaking previously saved objects. alabaster umbrella package pulls support common Bioconductor classes. Individual sub-packages cover specific classes: alabaster.se SummarizedExperiment, alabaster.sce SingleCellExperiment, . key part ArtifactDB design saved directories can independently validated format specification. handled takane, C++ library maintains separate, versioned specifications 30+ Bioconductor object types. Calling takane::validate() saved directory checks files conform expected layout types, means collaborator downstream tool can verify integrity saved object without needing load R. makes alabaster directories suitable deposition data repositories format conformance needs auditable. Python counterpart alabaster dolomite family packages, reads writes -disk format. object saved alabaster R can read dolomite Python, vice versa, conversion step. Advantages: Truly language-agnostic: Python readers exist via dolomite family packages, enabling seamless R ↔︎ Python interoperability. Built open standards (HDF5, JSON); inspectable without R. Versioned format specifications independent validation via takane. good choice archives data portals. Disadvantages: Newer ecosystem; Bioconductor classes alabaster support yet. Requires installing relevant alabaster.* sub-package class. Like HDF5Array, output directory. use : archival storage, data portal submissions, cross-language workflows, situation want saved format readable without R.
code: library(alabaster.base) library(alabaster.se)  tmp_alabaster <- tempfile() saveObject(se, path = tmp_alabaster)  se_from_alabaster <- readObject(tmp_alabaster) se_from_alabaster class: SummarizedExperiment dim: 3 4 metadata(0): assays(1): counts rownames(3): 1 2 3 rowData names(1): gene colnames(4): 1 2 3 4 colData names(1): condition

title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: object GRanges similar ranges object goal interoperability tools (genome browsers, Python, command-line utilities), exporting BED format often useful R-specific serialization. gr range names, standard BED score column, extra metadata column log2fc: rtracklayer plyranges can write BED files: reading back, names standard score column preserved, log2fc silently dropped — BED mechanism carry arbitrary metadata columns: round-trip extra mcols, write sidecar file. using plyranges read BED back, reattach sidecar: BED files store chromosome lengths genome build information, Seqinfo silently dropped export. preserve , write alongside BED file restore load:
code: gr GRanges object with 3 ranges and 2 metadata columns:         seqnames    ranges strand |     score    log2fc            <Rle> <IRanges>  <Rle> | <numeric> <numeric>   peak1     chr1   100-149      * |       500       1.2   peak2     chr1   200-249      * |       800      -0.5   peak3     chr1   300-349      * |       300       2.1   -------   seqinfo: 1 sequence from an unspecified genome; no seqlengths library(rtracklayer) library(plyranges)  tmp_bed <- tempfile(fileext = ".bed") export(gr, tmp_bed)  tmp_bed2 <- tempfile(fileext = ".bed") write_bed(gr, tmp_bed2) gr_rtracklayer <- import(tmp_bed) gr_rtracklayer GRanges object with 3 ranges and 2 metadata columns:       seqnames    ranges strand |        name     score          <Rle> <IRanges>  <Rle> | <character> <numeric>   [1]     chr1   100-149      * |       peak1       500   [2]     chr1   200-249      * |       peak2       800   [3]     chr1   300-349      * |       peak3       300   -------   seqinfo: 1 sequence from an unspecified genome; no seqlengths gr_plyranges <- read_bed(tmp_bed2) gr_plyranges GRanges object with 3 ranges and 2 metadata columns:       seqnames    ranges strand |        name     score          <Rle> <IRanges>  <Rle> | <character> <numeric>   [1]     chr1   100-149      * |       peak1       500   [2]     chr1   200-249      * |       peak2       800   [3]     chr1   300-349      * |       peak3       300   -------   seqinfo: 1 sequence from an unspecified genome; no seqlengths tmp_meta <- tempfile(fileext = ".tsv") write.table(   data.frame(name = names(gr), log2fc = gr$log2fc),   tmp_meta, sep = "\t", quote = FALSE, row.names = FALSE )  gr_restored <- read_bed(tmp_bed2) meta <- read.table(tmp_meta, header = TRUE, sep = "\t") gr_restored$log2fc <- meta$log2fc gr_restored GRanges object with 3 ranges and 3 metadata columns:       seqnames    ranges strand |        name     score    log2fc          <Rle> <IRanges>  <Rle> | <character> <numeric> <numeric>   [1]     chr1   100-149      * |       peak1       500       1.2   [2]     chr1   200-249      * |       peak2       800      -0.5   [3]     chr1   300-349      * |       peak3       300       2.1   -------   seqinfo: 1 sequence from an unspecified genome; no seqlengths tmp_seqinfo <- tempfile(fileext = ".csv") write.csv(as.data.frame(seqinfo(gr)), tmp_seqinfo)  df <- read.csv(tmp_seqinfo, row.names = 1) si <- Seqinfo(   seqnames   = rownames(df),   seqlengths = as.integer(df$seqlengths),   isCircular = as.logical(df$isCircular),   genome     = as.character(df$genome) ) si Seqinfo object with 1 sequence from an unspecified genome; no seqlengths:   seqnames seqlengths isCircular genome   chr1             NA         NA   <NA>

what: Saving metadata columns alongside a BED file
title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: round-trip extra mcols, write sidecar file. using plyranges read BED back, reattach sidecar:
code: tmp_meta <- tempfile(fileext = ".tsv") write.table(   data.frame(name = names(gr), log2fc = gr$log2fc),   tmp_meta, sep = "\t", quote = FALSE, row.names = FALSE )  gr_restored <- read_bed(tmp_bed2) meta <- read.table(tmp_meta, header = TRUE, sep = "\t") gr_restored$log2fc <- meta$log2fc gr_restored GRanges object with 3 ranges and 3 metadata columns:       seqnames    ranges strand |        name     score    log2fc          <Rle> <IRanges>  <Rle> | <character> <numeric> <numeric>   [1]     chr1   100-149      * |       peak1       500       1.2   [2]     chr1   200-249      * |       peak2       800      -0.5   [3]     chr1   300-349      * |       peak3       300       2.1   -------   seqinfo: 1 sequence from an unspecified genome; no seqlengths

title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: BED files store chromosome lengths genome build information, Seqinfo silently dropped export. preserve , write alongside BED file restore load:
code: tmp_seqinfo <- tempfile(fileext = ".csv") write.csv(as.data.frame(seqinfo(gr)), tmp_seqinfo)  df <- read.csv(tmp_seqinfo, row.names = 1) si <- Seqinfo(   seqnames   = rownames(df),   seqlengths = as.integer(df$seqlengths),   isCircular = as.logical(df$isCircular),   genome     = as.character(df$genome) ) si Seqinfo object with 1 sequence from an unspecified genome; no seqlengths:   seqnames seqlengths isCircular genome   chr1             NA         NA   <NA>

title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: Object-level metadata stored metadata(object) — things like processing parameters, provenance notes, experiment descriptors — lost format encodes ranges assay data. applies whether writing BED file, HDF5 matrix, non-R format. Write JSON sidecar file travels data: toJSON handles simple R types (lists, vectors, data frames) well, complex objects (S4 instances, environments) need simplified omitted serializing.
code: library(jsonlite)  metadata(se) <- list(genome = "hg38", pipeline = "v2.1", n_samples = 4L)  tmp_json <- tempfile(fileext = ".json") writeLines(toJSON(metadata(se), pretty = TRUE, auto_unbox = TRUE), tmp_json)  metadata(se_from_rds) <- fromJSON(tmp_json) metadata(se_from_rds) $genome [1] "hg38"  $pipeline [1] "v2.1"  $n_samples [1] 4

title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: new projects recommend defaulting saveRDS() convenience upgrading alabaster cross-language access archival stability becomes priority. aware R-serialized Bioconductor object may require updateObject() loaded different Bioconductor release.

title: savingBiocObjects
text: Documentation guidance strategies saving sharing Bioconductor objects. Topics covered: saveRDS() / readRDS() simple R--R sharing, cross-release stability HDF5-backed storage HDF5Array large assay data alabaster ecosystem language-agnostic serialization Converting SingleCellExperiment AnnData zellkonverter Exporting genomic ranges BED rtracklayer plyranges Preserving metadata(object) JSON sidecar file summary table recommendations use case
